title: Choosing K: How Many Clusters?
concept: Since K-Means requires K to be chosen in advance, the elbow method tests several K values and looks for the point where adding more clusters stops meaningfully improving the fit.

exercises.prompt: Recap: K-Means keeps looping through which two steps until things stabilize?

options.text: Assign points to centroids, then update centroids to the average of their points
Sort the data, then delete outliers
Label the data, then train a classifier

exercises.explain: Right! Assign, then update, repeated until the centroids barely move.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The number K in K-Means is discovered automatically by the algorithm as it runs.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! K must be chosen before K-Means starts — the algorithm doesn't figure it out on its own.

cards.title: How many pizzas for the party?
cards.body: Order too few pizzas and everyone's still hungry. Order way too many and you're wasting money on boxes nobody eats. There's a sweet spot where adding one more pizza barely helps anymore — and picking K in K-Means faces the exact same kind of trade-off.

cards.title: What is the elbow method?
cards.body: The elbow method picks K by running K-Means multiple times with different values of K, measuring how tightly the points fit their clusters each time, and plotting the results. As K increases, the fit keeps improving — but the elbow method looks for the point where those improvements suddenly start shrinking.

cards.title: Diminishing returns at the gym
cards.body: Going from 0 to 1 workout a week helps a lot. Going from 1 to 2 also helps noticeably. But going from 6 workouts a week to 7 barely moves the needle — you've hit diminishing returns. The elbow method looks for that same turning point: where more clusters stop paying off.

cards.title: Reading the elbow curve
cards.body: Picture a line chart with K (1, 2, 3, 4...) along the bottom and 'how spread out points are from their centroid' along the side. The line drops steeply from K=1 to K=3, then flattens out from K=4 onward. That bend — where steep drop turns into a flat line — looks like an elbow, and it points to a good choice of K, often K=3 or K=4 here.

cards.title: There's no single 'correct' K
cards.body: You might expect one mathematically perfect answer for K. In reality, the elbow method gives a reasonable range, not a guaranteed right answer — real elbows can be blurry, and the best K often also depends on what makes practical sense for the problem, like how many customer segments a marketing team can realistically act on.

cards.title: Too few clusters vs. too many

left.text:
Very different points get
crammed into one cluster

exercises.prompt: What is the goal of the elbow method?

options.text: To find a value of K where adding more clusters stops giving much extra improvement
To automatically remove outliers from the dataset
To speed up how fast K-Means runs
To pick which centroid should move first

exercises.explain: Correct! The elbow method finds where the improvement from a bigger K starts to level off.

exercises.prompt: True or false: On an elbow plot, the 'elbow' is where the curve stops dropping steeply and starts to flatten out.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! That bend from steep to flat is exactly what gives the elbow method its name.

exercises.sentence: The elbow method looks for the K value where increasing K gives ___ returns.
exercises.explain: Past the elbow, adding more clusters gives diminishing (shrinking) improvements.

exercises.prompt: A team tests K=2 through K=8 for a customer dataset and finds the fit improves a lot up to K=4, then barely improves at all after that. What should they likely choose?

options.text: K=4, since that's roughly where the elbow bend happens
K=8, because higher K is always better
K=1, to avoid clustering complexity entirely

exercises.explain: Right! K=4 sits at the elbow — past that point, extra clusters aren't earning their keep.

exercises.prompt: Why can't K-Means just try K=1000 and pick whichever K produces the tightest-fitting clusters?

options.text: Fit keeps improving as K grows, so the 'tightest fit' would just keep pointing to bigger and bigger K, even absurd ones like one cluster per point
K-Means physically cannot run with K greater than 10
Larger K values always produce worse-fitting clusters
Fit doesn't change at all as K increases

exercises.explain: Exactly! Fit almost always improves as K grows, so picking purely by 'tightest fit' would push K absurdly high — the elbow method balances fit against usefully few clusters.

exercises.prompt: A retailer runs the elbow method on shopper data and sees a smooth, gradual curve with no obvious bend anywhere from K=2 to K=10. What's the most reasonable conclusion?

options.text: The elbow is ambiguous here, so K should be chosen using a mix of a rough elbow estimate and practical business judgment, like how many shopper segments the marketing team can act on
The dataset is broken and clustering cannot be used
K should automatically default to 2, since that's the smallest tested value
The elbow method always produces a sharp, obvious bend, so this result must be a mistake

exercises.explain: Exactly! A blurry elbow is common in real data — practical judgment fills the gap the method leaves. Next: where K-Means actually starts to struggle!
